Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Edmund Husserl

1859 – 1938

Phenomenology as the rigorous science of consciousness in its intentional structure.

The most defensible reconstruction is this: Husserl’s philosophy of mind is a non-reductive, anti-naturalist, non-Cartesian, transcendental-phenomenological account of intentional, temporal, embodied, affective, volitional, intersubjective subjectivity. It is not a doctrine of a private mental substance inside the skull. It is also not physicalism, behaviorism, empiricist associationism, psychologism, or a speculative theology of incorporeal souls. Husserl treats “mind” through several registers: consciousness as intentional lived experience; the pure/transcendental ego as the pole and condition of world-disclosure; the psychic subject or soul as a worldly, embodied, animate stratum; and the person/spirit as the motivated, valuing, cultural, rational, historical subject.

The central thesis is that the mind is not first a thing. It is a field of lived, self-temporalizing, sense-bestowing activity in which objects, selves, bodies, others, values, and worlds appear with meaning and validity. But this field is not “inside” in the ordinary spatial sense; Husserl repeatedly tries to break the naive inside/outside picture of mind and world. In a concise contemporary formulation, the world for Husserl is neither reducible to consciousness nor separable from consciousness.

1. Orientation: the relevant corpus

Husserl’s mature view cannot be extracted from one book alone. The early Philosophy of Arithmetic still works close to descriptive psychology. The Logical Investigations rejects psychologism while developing the phenomenology of intentional acts. Ideas I introduces the transcendental reduction, the pure ego, noesis/noema, and the controversial language of “absolute consciousness.” Ideas II develops the body, psyche/soul, person, spirit, motivation, and the naturalistic versus personalistic attitudes. The lectures on internal time-consciousness, passive synthesis, phantasy, memory, and phenomenological psychology extend the picture. The Cartesian Meditations foreground intersubjectivity, and the Crisis diagnoses the failure of modern psychology when it imitates physics. The Leuven Husserl Archives list the English Husserliana volumes on Ideas II, internal time-consciousness, phenomenological psychology, passive and active synthesis, phantasy, memory, and related materials, which is a useful map of the relevant textual terrain.

Two textual cautions matter. First, Ideas II is indispensable for Husserl’s philosophy of mind, but it was published posthumously from manuscripts edited through Edith Stein and Ludwig Landgrebe; its textual history helps explain why some formulations seem more “naturalistic” or “dualistic” than others. Second, Husserl’s thought develops: the early anti-psychologistic project, the transcendental turn after 1907, the 1920s phenomenological psychology, and the late genetic/generative manuscripts do not all speak in exactly the same idiom.

2. Anti-psychologism: why Husserl rejects reducing logic and meaning to psychology

Husserl’s first decisive move is the critique of psychologism: the view that logic, mathematics, truth, and meaning can be grounded in empirical psychological facts or laws about how humans think. In the Logical Investigations, he argues that logical laws are not empirical generalizations about mental events. A judgment-act is a real temporal event; its ideal meaning or proposition is not. Confusing these levels corrupts both logic and psychology.

This anti-psychologism is not anti-psychology. Husserl’s point is that empirical psychology cannot ground ideal validity, but philosophy still needs rigorous descriptions of judging, perceiving, imagining, valuing, remembering, and willing. The early Logical Investigations even called phenomenology a kind of “descriptive psychology,” but Husserl later judged this inadequate because phenomenology must also examine the constitutive correlation between consciousness and objectivity.

The importance for philosophy of mind is considerable. Husserl distinguishes three things that many theories collapse: the real mental act; the intentional object meant in that act; and the ideal or noematic sense by which the object is meant. A thought is a lived event, but what it thinks is not reducible to that event’s psychological occurrence.

3. The method: natural attitude, epoché, phenomenological reduction, eidetic analysis

Husserl begins from the natural attitude, the ordinary and scientific stance in which the world is simply taken as existing. The epoché does not deny the world; it suspends or brackets the naive positing of the world in order to examine how world-validity is given. The phenomenological reduction redirects inquiry from objects simply taken as there to the correlation between objectivity and the acts, horizons, syntheses, and evidences through which it is disclosed. The IEP summary rightly emphasizes that Husserl’s method brackets the natural attitude and turns to the structures of consciousness rather than treating consciousness as one empirical object among others.

This gives two related but distinct projects.

Phenomenological psychology studies psychic life in its purity, but it remains within the world: the psychologist is still concerned with human or animal soul-life. Husserl says that for a “pure phenomenological psychology” one can study pure conscious life without fully adopting the transcendental attitude; the world remains in validity, although the psychologist abstracts from bodily-worldly matters in order to describe “pure innerness.”

Transcendental phenomenology goes further. It does not study the soul as a worldly reality. It asks how world, nature, body, others, psyche, culture, and science acquire sense and validity for transcendental subjectivity. German discussions of the 1920s lectures stress this difference: phenomenological psychology remains tied to the natural world and conscious relation to it, whereas transcendental phenomenology asks after the constitution of all worldly givenness in transcendental subjectivity and intersubjectivity.

So Husserl’s “mind” has at least two levels: the mundane psyche, which belongs to humans and animals in the world, and transcendental subjectivity, which is not another object in the world but the dimension in which any world-objectivity becomes meaningful.

4. Intentionality: the mark and structure of the mental

Husserl inherits from Brentano the thesis that consciousness is characterized by intentionality, object-directedness. But he transforms it. Intentionality is not a mysterious relation between an inner mental item and an outer thing. It is the structure by which something appears as something: perceived as a tree, remembered as yesterday’s walk, judged as true, desired as good, feared as dangerous, imagined as possible.

In the Logical Investigations, Husserl analyzes acts through distinctions such as act-quality, act-matter, and act-character. The quality concerns whether the act is judging, questioning, wishing, fearing, and so on. The matter concerns the object as meant and the way it is meant. The character concerns positionality: whether the object is posited as actual, merely imagined, doubtful, wished-for, and so forth. He also distinguishes empty intentions from fulfilled intentions: I can mean “the back of the house” without seeing it, and later the intention can be fulfilled by perception.

This yields a rich philosophy of mental content. Husserlian content is not simply a sentence in the head, a neural representation, or a private image. It is a noematic sense embedded in horizons of possible fulfillment, correction, and further experience. His later terminology distinguishes noesis, the act-side or experiencing-side, from noema, the object-as-meant or sense-side; scholarship still debates whether the noema should be interpreted more like a Fregean sense, an abstract object, the intentional object under a mode of presentation, or a structural moment of experience.

The crucial point is that consciousness is not a container of mental pictures. Perception, for Husserl, reaches the thing itself, though only through profiles, adumbrations, and horizons. In Ideas I, he rejects picture-theories and sign-theories of perception: the perceived thing is not replaced by an inner image or sign; it is given “in person” in the manner proper to perception.

5. Horizon, adumbration, and perceptual objectivity

Every perception is partial, perspectival, and horizonal. I see one side of a cup, but the unseen sides are co-intended. I perceive a thing as the same thing through changing appearances. The object is not an aggregate of sensations; it is an identity synthesized across profiles and possible future confirmations.

Husserl’s notion of horizon is one of his major contributions to philosophy of mind. Any actual experience points beyond itself to possible experiences, and these possibilities are not arbitrary but motivated and typified. In Ideas I, he says that experience is surrounded by a determinable horizon and that actual experience points to possible experiences “ad infinitum.”

This makes Husserl neither a naive realist nor a sense-datum theorist. He is a kind of phenomenological direct realist about perception’s intentional directedness: we perceive the object itself, but the object itself is given only through structured profiles, anticipations, and possible fulfillments.

6. Time-consciousness: the stream, retention, protention, and pre-reflective self-awareness

For Husserl, mind is essentially temporal. Consciousness is not a sequence of punctual now-points. Each living present has a threefold structure: primal impression of what is now, retention of what has just elapsed, and protention of what is about to come. Retention is not ordinary memory; it is the immediate holding-on of the just-past. Protention is not explicit prediction; it is the anticipatory openness of experience.

This explains how we hear a melody rather than isolated tones, how a perception persists as one perception through change, and how the self is not an object added to experience but is implicated in the flow of experiencing. The stream is self-manifesting before reflective introspection. Reflection can turn back upon an experience, but the experience was already lived through in a first-personal way.

This also gives Husserl a distinctive answer to the unity of consciousness. Unity is not imposed by a substance behind experience; nor is it merely a bundle. It is achieved through temporal synthesis, habituality, bodily orientation, and the ego-pole of acts.

7. The ego: pure ego, ego-pole, habit, person

Husserl’s treatment of the ego evolves. In the Logical Investigations he was skeptical of a pure ego, but in Ideas I he retracts that skepticism and treats the pure ego as a peculiar “transcendency within immanency,” not something to be excluded by the reduction.

The Husserlian ego is not simply a Cartesian mental substance. It has several layers.

At the transcendental level, the pure ego is the pole of intentional acts, the “I” to which experiences are oriented.

At the genetic level, the ego is a system of habitualities: sedimented convictions, skills, styles of attention, capacities, and tendencies.

At the personal level, the ego is a concrete person with character, values, projects, and a life-history.

At the embodied practical level, the ego is an “I can”: a system of powers, abilities, and possibilities of action. In Ideas II, Husserl explicitly analyzes the ego through bodily and spiritual capacities, skill acquisition and loss, normality, individual powers, self-education, and ethical transformation.

This is one of the most important points for a Husserlian philosophy of mind: subjectivity is not exhausted by occurrent conscious episodes. It includes latent abilities, dispositions, affective tendencies, bodily skills, and personal commitments.

8. Passive synthesis, affectivity, and the unconscious

Husserl is sometimes misread as a philosopher only of active, lucid, reflective consciousness. The later manuscripts and lectures show the opposite. He develops analyses of passive synthesis, association, affection, salience, habit, instinct, sleep, and unconsciousness. The Leuven Husserliana list includes volumes on passive and active synthesis, phantasy, image-consciousness, memory, and other forms of non-straightforward presence.

Passive synthesis means that experience is already organized before explicit judgment. Objects stand out, attract attention, recede, become familiar, sediment into habits, and motivate expectations. The mind is not first an active judge applying concepts to raw data. It is an affectively and temporally organized field in which salience, association, and bodily orientation already operate.

Husserliana XLII is especially relevant here. Its German title identifies “limit-problems of phenomenology,” including the unconscious, instincts, birth, sleep, death, metaphysics, teleology, philosophical theology, and late ethics. A review of that volume notes that these problems concern breaches or limits in ordinary givenness and push phenomenology beyond simple static description.

A Husserlian philosophy of mind therefore has room for the unconscious, but not primarily as a hidden causal mechanism in the Freudian or neuroscientific sense. The “unconscious” is better understood as latency, passivity, sedimentation, instinctive directedness, and the horizonal background of possible awakening, recollection, affective arousal, and motivation.

9. Embodiment: Leib, Körper, kinesthesia, and the “I can”

Husserl’s mature philosophy of mind is embodied. The key distinction is between Körper, the body as physical thing, and Leib, the lived body. The lived body is not merely an object I observe; it is the medium of my access to objects, the zero-point of orientation, the organ of perception, the bearer of localized sensations, and the organ of will.

The lived body is the “here” from which every “there” is oriented. Things appear near or far, left or right, reachable or unreachable, in relation to my embodied situation. In Ideas II, Husserl says material qualities depend on relation to the experiencing subject-body, and he describes the body as medium or organ of all perception, with kinesthetic sensations and a zero-point of orientation.

Kinesthesia is central. Perception is tied to possible movement: if I move my eyes, head, hands, or body, the object’s profiles change in lawful ways. Thus perceptual consciousness is not a passive reception of data but a bodily, sensorimotor, horizonal relation to things.

Touch is especially revealing. The touching hand can also be touched; in double-touch, the body appears both as sensing subject and sensed object. The body becomes a lived body through localized sensations such as touch and pain; it is also an organ of will, immediately and spontaneously moveable by the “I can.”

This is why Husserl is a key precursor of embodied cognition. But his embodiment is not a reduction of mind to physiology. The lived body is neither a mere physical object nor an immaterial mind’s external instrument. It is the psychophysical, affective, kinesthetic, practical field in which subjectivity is worldly.

10. Are thoughts in the head? Husserl’s anti-localization thesis

Husserl makes an especially striking point in Ideas II: bodily feelings, pains, pleasures, desires, and many affective sensations are localized in the lived body, but intentional acts as such are not localized in the same way. He says that total consciousness is bound to the body through a hyletic substrate, yet intentional acts are not literally localized; “thinking in the head” is not a straightforward phenomenological fact but a metaphorical or derivative way of speaking.

This is highly relevant to contemporary philosophy of mind. Husserl would resist the simple claim that a thought is located in the brain in the same phenomenological sense in which a pain is felt in the hand. He does not deny bodily and organic conditions of mental life. He denies that the sense, object-directedness, and intentional structure of thinking can be understood by treating thoughts as spatial items contained in the skull.

11. Psyche, soul, and the psychic subject

Husserl’s term Seele is often translated as “soul,” but it usually means the psyche or psychic life of a human or animal, not an immortal immaterial substance in the theological sense. In the Crisis, Husserl explicitly says that “soul” should be understood not metaphysically but according to the original givenness of the psychic in the life-world.

In Ideas II, the psychic ego is not identical with the mere flux of experiences. It is a unity related to that flux: it has, lives, and lives in experiences. Psychic properties are not isolated inner atoms; they are unities of manifestation, dispositions, character-traits, and personal properties grounded in actual and possible lived experiences.

The soul is embodied. Husserl says the psychic subject has a material thing as body because that body is animated. The body is not added externally to an already complete soul; rather, psychic life becomes worldly, located, and experientially accessible through the lived body.

A strong Spanish reading makes this explicit: Husserl distinguishes the pure or transcendental ego from the real psychic subject or soul; “soul” in Ideas II refers to psyche linked with the human or animal body, and the lived body is a peculiar unity between material nature and spirit. Italian reception similarly stresses that psychic being must be connected with the lived body and that the psychical subject has a material thing as lived body because it is animated by psychic life.

12. Nature, soul, spirit: the stratified ontology of the person

Husserl’s ontology of the human being is stratified. At minimum, he distinguishes:

Nature or materiality: the body as physical thing, causal processes, spatial-temporal material being.

Soul or psyche: animate, sensitive, affective, perceptual, desiring, embodied psychic life.

Spirit or personhood: the level of motivation, reasons, values, culture, community, language, responsibility, and historical life.

Spanish scholarship on Ideas II describes this as the constitution of nature, soul, and spirit from the ego, with the relation between the psychic subject and spiritual subject central to intersubjectivity and the life-world. Italian Treccani likewise notes that after 1907 Husserl develops regional ontologies of space, the lived body, material thing, soul, psychological acts, intersubjectivity, society, and ethics.

The crucial explanatory principle for spirit is motivation, not physical causality. In a Spanish translation of Husserl’s Freiburg lecture on nature and spirit, motivation is described as the great principle of order in the proper psychic world and then in the whole spiritual world, just as causality is the principle of the order of nature.

This is not a denial of causal bodily conditions. It is a distinction of explanatory attitudes. A headache may have neurological causes; my apology, promise, belief, shame, or decision must also be understood through reasons, meanings, norms, and motivations. Husserl’s philosophy of mind is therefore pluralistic about explanation.

13. Naturalistic and personalistic attitudes

Husserl distinguishes the naturalistic attitude, which abstracts from value, use, culture, and personhood in order to consider bodies and psychophysical beings as nature, from the personalistic attitude, in which we encounter persons, tools, artworks, institutions, obligations, and meanings in the life-world.

The naturalistic attitude is legitimate when limited. Husserl does not reject natural science. A German study of Husserl on the soul as natural object stresses that Husserl does not deny the right of the natural-scientific attitude; he rejects its absolutization into the only source of truth about everything.

This is a key point for philosophy of mind. Husserl is not saying that psychology, biology, or neuroscience are useless. He is saying that they become confused when they forget the life-world and treat consciousness as if its essence were exhausted by the categories appropriate to physical things.

14. Husserl’s critique of modern psychology

In the Crisis, Husserl argues that modern psychology failed because it modeled itself on physics. It treated the soul as a psychophysical reality analogous to corporeal nature, thereby missing the original givenness of psychic life.

His critique is subtle. He does not deny empirical discoveries. He says empirical psychology can discover many facts about human soul-life, but it does not thereby grasp the mind’s own essence if it reifies the psychic into quasi-physical data or causal atoms.

The psychologist must begin from how souls are given in the life-world: how they animate living bodies, how each lives in consciousness of a world, how each experiences its body as a lived body and system of organs, and how each acts through “I strike,” “I push,” “I lift.”

This gives Husserl’s philosophy of mind a methodological demand: before explaining consciousness causally, psychology must clarify the sense of consciousness, intentionality, embodiment, motivation, selfhood, and intersubjectivity.

15. The life-world and the critique of scientific objectivism

Husserl’s late critique of science is not anti-science. His claim is that modern science, especially Galilean mathematized physics, forgets the pre-scientific life-world from which its own concepts derive their sense. In the Crisis, he says the everyday world is given subjectively-relative through changing appearances while remaining the one world of experience.

Galileo is both a discovering and concealing genius: mathematical science discovers exact lawfulness while concealing the subjective-relative life-world that makes scientific idealization meaningful.

For mind and psychology, the consequence is decisive. If psychology borrows the model of physics without clarifying the life-worldal givenness of psyche, body, person, and motivation, it misconstrues its own subject matter.

16. Other minds: empathy, expression, and intersubjectivity

Husserl’s theory of other minds is not an inference from private inner data to hidden foreign minds. He treats empathy as a distinctive intentional structure in which another lived body is experienced as the body of another subject. The other’s experience is not given originarily as mine is, but the other is not a mere hypothesis.

The other’s body is expressive. I do not first perceive a moving physical object and then infer anger, pain, joy, or attention. In ordinary experience, gestures, posture, gaze, voice, and action already appear as meaningful expressions of psychic life.

There remains an asymmetry: I do not live through the other’s pain as the other does. Yet this asymmetry is not a skeptical barrier. It is part of the structure of empathy. Husserl also argues that others are essential for objectivity: a world is not merely my private stream but a common world open to verification, correction, and shared orientation.

Italian reception emphasizes that the lived body is the center of an environment and that other egos are reached as foreign lived bodies through empathy; this allows different subjects to have the same world despite the first-personal ownership of lived experiences.

17. Sociality, history, and culture

Husserl’s mature subject is not a solitary Cartesian spectator. Transcendental subjectivity is intersubjective, historical, and generative. The life-world is culturally shaped; meanings are sedimented in language, institutions, practices, traditions, and sciences.

This matters because mental life is not merely perception plus belief. It includes normativity, tradition, communicability, ideal objects, shared practices, and historically transmitted senses. A Husserlian philosophy of mind therefore includes social cognition, cultural cognition, and norm-governed rationality as basic topics, not secondary additions.

18. Soul and incorporeal mind: what Husserl does and does not say

Husserl’s mature position is not a simple doctrine of an incorporeal finite soul. The mundane soul is embodied, localized through the lived body, and dependent on psychophysical unity. In the Crisis, Husserl warns against understanding soul metaphysically and criticizes psychology for inheriting a Cartesian concept of soul modeled as a quasi-corporeal reality.

However, Ideas I contains passages that easily look like radical dualism or idealism. Husserl says that the existence of a world is the correlate of ordered multiplicities of experience, and he develops the famous thought experiment of “absolute consciousness as the residuum after the annihilation of the world.” He argues that the annihilation of the world would modify consciousness but would not touch its own existence.

This passage should not be read too quickly as “Husserl believes in ghostly minds floating free of bodies.” In context, the argument concerns the difference between immanent lived experience and transcendent worldly reality, and the methodological discovery of pure consciousness as a field of phenomenological research. Husserl’s later analyses of embodiment, intersubjectivity, passivity, birth, death, and life-world complicate any simple disembodied reading.

God is also not part of ordinary phenomenological psychology. In Ideas I, Husserl treats God as a peculiar transcendence that must be excluded from the field of pure phenomenological research, even while acknowledging teleological questions. Later manuscripts do discuss philosophical theology, teleology, monadology, birth, death, and immortality-like themes, but these belong to Husserl’s limit-problems and metaphysical borderlands rather than to a straightforward empirical theory of incorporeal minds.

There are late and controversial passages on monads and immortality. A dissertation on Husserl’s phenomenology of death reports that in texts around Husserliana XLII Husserl speaks of monads as immortal or indestructible, and interprets this as tied to the problem of maintaining the primacy of transcendental subjectivity without positing nature as an in-itself prior to consciousness. The same study distinguishes a metaphysical reading from a more methodological or metaphorical reading and notes that the topic is speculative and contested.

So the balanced conclusion is this: Husserl does not offer a standard doctrine of immortal incorporeal souls. He does offer a transcendental account in which consciousness is not a worldly physical thing, and he sometimes speaks in ways that invite metaphysical monadological readings. But his phenomenology of the soul, psyche, and person is overwhelmingly tied to embodiment, life-world, motivation, and intersubjectivity.

19. Multilingual reception: several non-Anglophone emphases

The German reception is especially attentive to the difference between natural attitude, naturalistic attitude, phenomenological psychology, and transcendental phenomenology. The Meixner source is useful because it shows how Ideas II may appear to contain a kind of naturalized “substance dualism per analogiam” when Husserl temporarily adopts a naturalistic attitude, while the Phenomenological Psychology lectures more strongly deny the soul’s concrete independence from embodiment.

French reception often focuses on chair, Leib, and the relation between soul and flesh. Pierre Lorelle’s article argues against a simple story in which Ideas II merely moves from sensible body to spirit; he proposes that Husserl also defines the lived body through properties borrowed from the soul, even a “spiritualization” of flesh. French discussions of Husserl and the sciences also highlight the relation between intentional psychology and transcendental phenomenology, and recent French commentary treats Husserl’s foundation of psychology as a clarification of mental concepts still relevant for philosophy of mind.

Spanish reception is often strong on alma, cuerpo vivo, and subjetividad trascendental encarnada. The Scielo article on the living body and transcendental subjectivity argues that Ideas II shows the transcendental subject as embodied, historical, concrete, teleological, communal, and not solipsistically isolated. It also stresses Husserl’s distinction between the transcendental ego and the real psychic subject or soul.

Italian reception often emphasizes the overcoming of mind-body dualism through the relation between psiche and corpo vivo. Bandiera’s study argues that the psychic subject is not primarily related to the body as a mere material thing; rather, the subject has a material thing as lived body because that body is animated and intimately fused with psychic lived experiences. Angela Ales Bello’s Italian work likewise stresses the intertwining of living body and psyche, including localized sensations, affective movements, hyletic bases of value, and the body as a fundamental basis for the givenness of psyche and psychic ego.

The comparative lesson from this multilingual reception is that Anglophone discussions sometimes over-center intentional content, noema, and anti-psychologism, while French, Spanish, Italian, and German discussions often bring out the body-soul-spirit architecture, lived body, motivation, and the methodological status of psychology more vividly.

20. Extracted systematic philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Husserlian philosophy of mind can be formulated in the following theses.

First, the mental is fundamentally intentional. Consciousness is consciousness of something as something. Mental life is structured by object-directedness, sense, horizon, fulfillment, evidence, and possible correction.

Second, mental content is not reducible to private images, neural states, or linguistic propositions. Husserlian content is noematic, horizonal, and embedded in possible experience.

Third, consciousness is temporally self-constituting. Retention, primal impression, and protention make possible continuity, identity, anticipation, memory, melody, action, and self-awareness.

Fourth, the self is not a bare substance. It is an ego-pole, a stream, a system of habitualities, an embodied “I can,” and a person with character, history, values, and commitments.

Fifth, embodiment is constitutive, not accidental. The lived body is the zero-point of orientation, organ of perception, field of localized sensation, and organ of will.

Sixth, the psyche or soul is worldly and embodied. Husserl’s soul is not primarily an incorporeal ghost but the animate psychic stratum of human and animal life, linked with the lived body.

Seventh, personal life is governed by motivation as well as causality. Human action, belief, emotion, valuation, and ethical life require explanation by reasons, values, and sense-relations, not only by physical causes.

Eighth, other minds are given through embodied empathy and expression. They are neither directly lived as mine nor merely inferred as hidden causes.

Ninth, objectivity is intersubjective. A world is a world for actual and possible subjects, open to shared verification, correction, tradition, and communication.

Tenth, empirical psychology is legitimate but incomplete unless grounded in phenomenological clarification. A psychology that imitates physics without analyzing intentionality, embodiment, motivation, and life-world misconstrues the psychic.

Eleventh, the mind-body problem is transformed. Husserl does not solve it by reducing mind to body or by positing two independent substances. He re-situates it through the lived body, psychophysical unity, motivation, and the distinction between transcendental subjectivity and worldly psyche.

Twelfth, metaphysical questions about God, immortal monads, birth, death, sleep, and the unconscious are not absent, but they belong to the border-problems of phenomenology. They should not be projected backward as the core of Husserl’s philosophy of mind.

21. Final assessment

Husserl’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a layered theory of subjectivity. At the most basic level, there is temporal, affective, passive, intentional consciousness. At the bodily level, subjectivity is kinesthetic, localized, practical, and capable. At the psychic level, it is soul-life: embodied, dispositional, affective, perceptual, desiring, and animal. At the personal-spiritual level, it is motivated, valuing, rational, ethical, cultural, and historical. At the transcendental level, it is the condition for the sense and validity of worldhood itself.

The resulting view is neither Cartesian dualism nor materialist reduction. Husserl gives us a philosophy of mind in which mind is not an inner object but the living, embodied, temporal, intersubjective disclosure of a meaningful world.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
